title: Angled Footstool for Adirondack Chair
summary: A slatted outdoor ottoman angled to match an Adirondack chair's seat, so your legs land level. One 2x4, one 1x4, an hour.
description:
An Adirondack chair reclines, so a flat footstool leaves your feet tipped at the wrong angle. This little stool fixes that: the stringers are cut at 30° so the top slopes to meet your outstretched legs comfortably, matching the chair's recline.

It's the simplest possible outdoor build — two angled stringers, five deck slats, and two angled legs — but the angle is the whole point, so cut the 30° ends carefully and consistently. Everything else is just screwing slats down.

Build it from cedar or another rot-resistant wood if it stays outside, and use exterior deck screws so nothing rusts or streaks. The slats get a small gap between them to drain rain, same as the chair it partners.

tools.note: The 30° stringer and leg cuts are what set the recline angle.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Stringers and legs. Cedar or another rot-resistant species for outdoors. Stud length is fine.

cutList.note: Both ends cut 30° off square, parallel to each other, long point to short point.
One end cut 30° off square, longest-point measurement.

steps.title: Cut and trim the stringers
steps.body: Cut the two stringers to length with both ends at 30° off square, parallel to each other. This angle is what tilts the finished top to meet your legs — cut both identical.

steps.title: Attach the deck slats
steps.body: Lay the five 1x4 slats across the two stringers with even gaps between them for drainage, and attach with screws or nails. Keep the ends aligned.

steps.title: Attach the legs and flip
steps.body: Attach the two angled legs to the underside, then flip the stool right-side up. Ease any sharp edges and it's ready. Match it to your Adirondack chair and put your feet up.

images.alt: A slatted outdoor footstool angled to match an Adirondack chair
